title: AI can be trusted with meaningful delegated responsibility
description: An AI system can integrate many capabilities into one governed, monitorable, and dependable system that institutions would plausibly trust with serious responsibility.

subQuestions.title: Can it perform reliably across varied role types?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether one coherent system can cover a broad set of meaningful roles rather than a narrow slice of tasks.
subQuestions.rationale: Capability breadth is increasing, but dependable cross-role performance remains far from demonstrated in the real world.

proofItems.title: Deployment audit: Cross-role reliability in real deployments
proofItems.shortExplanation: Deployment inventories show wider assistant use, but not reliable performance across a broad portfolio of meaningful delegated roles.

subQuestions.title: Can it integrate multiple capabilities into one coherent system?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether reasoning, learning, tool use, grounding, and safety behaviors remain aligned when combined in one operational system.
subQuestions.rationale: Integrated systems are increasingly impressive, but capability composition still creates brittleness, especially over longer runs.

proofItems.title: Deployment audit: Integrated capability coherence
proofItems.shortExplanation: System audits show increasingly capable end-to-end behavior, but also document brittle interactions once many capabilities are composed together.

subQuestions.title: Can it be monitored and governed after the fact?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether institutions can reconstruct, inspect, and govern the system's actions in a practically meaningful way.
subQuestions.rationale: Current governance tooling is still too partial and too workflow-specific for broad delegated responsibility.

proofItems.title: Deployment audit: Post-hoc governance and monitoring quality
proofItems.shortExplanation: Governance audits show some usable traces and controls, but not enough coverage or reliability for broad institutional delegation.

subQuestions.title: Can it avoid severe failures in high-impact settings?
subQuestions.description: Tests tail-risk behavior rather than average-case competence under ordinary conditions.
subQuestions.rationale: Evidence on rare but severe failure prevention remains too weak for broad trust in high-impact delegation.

proofItems.title: Red-team evaluation: Severe-failure resistance in high-impact scenarios
proofItems.shortExplanation: Tail-risk evaluations continue to find too much uncertainty and too many unresolved severe-failure modes for broad delegation.

subQuestions.title: Can it show strong evidence from real deployments, not only lab wins?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether serious institutions have already delegated meaningful responsibility in practice, with convincing evidence of success.
subQuestions.rationale: Real deployment evidence is still dominated by bounded copilots, pilots, and supervised assistance rather than genuine responsibility transfer.

proofItems.title: Deployment audit: Real-world evidence for meaningful delegation
proofItems.shortExplanation: Deployment reviews show expanding use, but not the sustained, high-responsibility institutional delegation that this threshold would require.
